other_observers: Kara Lewantowicz; I am the original finder and idenifier of this individual
description: This bird was a very large flycatcher in the genus Empidonax. I had been seeing many Empids all May including other Alder Flycatchers that were not as cooperative to photograph. This bird was identified by call but a description (see photo) is included because this bird would probably have been identified as Willow Flycatcher if it did not call, as the back was not as green on its back, as other Alder Flycatchers seen in May at the same location, Chico Basin Ranch. Willow Flycatcher were also heard (sing and call) and seen the same day but not at this exact location.  Features that stand out are the complete small eyering, the peaked crown on the dark head, the large wide, orangish bill, the longish winged, longish tailed Empidonax. Once again this bird was identified by call which could be heard at close range (see photo for closeness).Behaviors: Flycatching low in willows and also a medium height in plains cottonwoods and siberian elms. Foraged alone. Perched often in plain view but mostly within a circle of branch so that although it often perched in the open it was surrounded by foliage in the bigger picture.Call: loud pip heard as many as 8 times, one very loud explosive call note possibly the one described by Sibley as a low, clear, pewPlumage: alternate
